post.content:
Hi,

I was asked the following question during the system design interview. 

A system that injects messages from other services. Plus, analzes this data.
- The volume can't fit on a single disk
- The volume can't be handeled by a single server
- Messages can come out of order (End event can come before start event)

Supported APIs:
```
startEvent(event_id, timestamp)
endEvent(event_id, timestamp)
getOngoingEvents(timestamp)
```

These `startEvent` and `endEvent` endpoints basically get an update from an event. Therefore, they need to store these timestamps. On the other hand, `getOngoingEvents` is a read operation. It should return number of events that are started already but not ended yet. 


I was thinking a K/V database to have a table like `event_id, start_date, end_date`. Where on each event if can create/update a/the record (constraint #3)

When calculating the getOngoingEvent, the query should look like this:
```
select count(*)
from events
where start_date < timestamp < end_date
```

I also mentioned a TimeSeries DB here instead of a K/V DB. However, I have not much idea of what to index and how TS DB's work much.

However, what's the best way to add sharding/indexing here? Any other thoughts or solutions are appreciated.

Solution to get count of ongoing events: Maintain two kafka topics, one for startEvent and another for endEvent. Consumers consume both these topics. Whenever there is a startEvent, the consumer increments the counter(initially set to zero) and when there is an endEvent, the consumer decrements the counter. The counter is stored in a db, say redis. Ofcourse we need handle the concurrent modification at the consumer level, so we need to to use something that supports atomic increment/decrement(Redis supports atomic incr), rather than using read-modify-write to update the counter.\n

Based on the limited input and the fact that this is an interview at Uber, I\'m assuming that this is Uber\'s system for keeping track of the number of rides going on at any given time. The first thing to understand is that startEvent and endEvent are writes into a DB. startevent is generated when a ride starts and endEvent when the ride ends and each have an associated event Id. The query to count the number of events ongoing is asking for events that have a start but no end yet. \n\nGiven that my table would have 3 columns timestamp, event type, event Id. The primary key can be event Id + event Type assuming event Id\'s are unique. For searching, I would secondary index on timestamp. I would use sharding for dividing this table into multiple machines. The key for sharding would be the event ID. This would keep the save events end and start times on the same shard/machine. This makes each shard/machine independent. Infact, with this schema you can have different databases storing this table independently and all you need is some kind of map/reduce or aggregator to count the total number of on ongoing rides.

Not sure why the link from earlier was taken down.  It had some pretty helpful coverage from a FAANG senior engineer.

EDIT: Re-found it.  Attached below.

3 or 4 different designs were covered, the following screenshots of the final diagrams were attached: https://imgur.com/a/Aox0mnZ
The excalidraw diagram that was worked on throughout the video was also linked and included somewhere:
- https://excalidraw.com/#json=nRB-aDQW0CXD9XF4ukL9V,TLMyJpHIO21zA8ygZOfzEA
- https://excalidraw.com/#json=XaaT0XA8U1tcXZBVClzi9,9oiH1RzobSYTjDF9eK2rCw

And here's the original video link from earlier that was removed for some reason

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/p-MNmIdEBFM" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen></iframe>

The APIs are not clearly defined. Locking the scope is half the problem. Here are my questions to the interviewee/(er)
1) `startEvent` and `endEvent` appear to be GET APIs based on the statement `... basically get an update from an event` And it also says it needs to store these timestamps, so this is a service to service call. Essentially you are locking yourself to a http construct without considering other options. Did the interviewer ask it to be a http verb?
2) As other's have pointed out, what's the latency expected, what's the data access pattern? Designing a system for 10M+ start, end Events and 100s of getOngoingEvents is very different from 1M+ start, end Events and 1M getOngoingEvents. Was this locked down?
3) What success means for this system - how did you arrive at sharding / indexing is the problem to solve here? Unless you have domain expertise in TimeSeries DB, it is not expected to answer about the internals of a TS DB. IMHO, this looks like solving the distributed-ness of the query pattern more important than the storage side. Ofc, I'm just guessing here, the interviewer may have had other ideas.
Any material on which you based your solution on? I have never heard of such design requirement before. Most I have seen are design a parking garage.

Any opinions on this solutions\n\nlets store a set of started event in redis cluster with say 5 nodes. The cluster is sharded on event_id. Writing becomes a add operation in a set of a single node, Upon recieving the end event for a given event id we can remove the entry from the set. So essentially the total number of event will be the sum of sizes of set of all nodes. This is also scatter-gather in action but if required we can have a read service which can calculate this result every 10 milisec. This way we can read and write with high throughput.

Keep and event table with the schema : event_id, start_ts, end_ts. Create a db trigger to update the ongoing count on a separate redis instance . The trigger will have the following logic:

If start_ts is received before end_ts increment redis counter.
When end_ts is received after start_ts decrement redis_counter.

If end_ts is received without start_ts do nothing.
and if start_ts is received after end_ts do nothing.

This way we can shard the db cluster on event id for an even load, sharding on ts will lead to hot partitions for some timestamps.

Need more information but a RDBMS with debezium or a RDBMS with Kafka, or a KV store with Kafka or ... etc. There's a million ways to do this. Also, your query won't work since end_date wouldn't be set for ongoing events. Youll need something like
```
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE start_date < timestamp AND end_date IS NULL
```

I think having a distributed list of startTime event and endTime event in memory would be useful. If the list gets affected then we can get the data from the db as you suggested and build it again. And pop from the startTime list once you get the endTime, or vice versa. The list of the startTime list would give you the number of ongoing trips.

Basic idea can sharding based on event start time. We can create a shard for every let's say 5 hour(depending on the duration we want to store the data for). So 00hr - 04 hr on 1 shard and 05-09hr on another shard. 
Whenever a startEvent message comes we can directly refer the shard and update/insert the row for that event 
Whenever a endEvent message comes we can directly refer the shard or next shard best case (assuming the sharding interval is much bigger than the event duration) and update/insert the row for that event.
Whenever a getOngoingEvent comes we can check the shard by the start timestamp.
This looks like a good strategy. Feedback is most welcome.
Can you elaborate more on what those APIs do? are those READ or WRITE APIs?\n
For the solution, try aonecode design 1v1 session. You can ask any topics.
Given the requirements and constraints, we can design a distributed system to handle the volume and achieve the desired functionality.
